summary: Gruber corrects the common misconception that Return and Enter are synonyms, prompted by a New York Times crossword clue treating them as interchangeable. He explains that they are two physically distinct keys with different key codes and glyphs on Mac keyboards, with Enter typically found only on extended keyboards with numeric keypads. While they usually perform the same action, several Mac apps distinguish between them — Return inserts a newline while Enter confirms or executes input. The post serves as a concise technical explainer rooted in Gruber's characteristic attention to overlooked details.
key_points: Return (key code 36, glyph ↵) and Enter (key code 76, glyph ⌅) are physically and functionally distinct keys on Mac keyboards
On keyboards without a dedicated Enter key, Fn-Return produces the Enter keystroke
Several Mac apps (Tweetbot, Script Editor, BBEdit) use the distinction meaningfully: Return inserts a newline, Enter confirms or executes
The general rule is that Return types a newline character, while Enter submits or confirms what you've already typed
money_quotes: They're two different keys that usually perform the same action, but not always.
If your keyboard has neither a dedicated Enter key nor an Fn modifier key, I don't think you can type Enter, without resorting to sorcery.
Just look at such a keyboard: the Return key says "Return", and the Enter key says "Enter".
As a general rule, when they differ, Return is simply the key for typing a newline character, whereas Enter enters what you've already typed without adding a new line.
In Tweetbot for Mac, when you're editing a tweet, Return will insert a newline character; Enter will immediately send the tweet.
key_insight: Return and Enter are distinct keys with different key codes and behaviors — Return inserts a newline while Enter confirms input — and conflating them overlooks a meaningful design distinction in computing.
